title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
description: Back to 1D DP, but with a twist: dp[i] depends on checking EVERY earlier index, not just one or two fixed ones.
objectives: Solve the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem using 1D dynamic programming
Define a DP state as 'best ending exactly here'
Understand why LIS needs an inner loop over all earlier indices
Recognize the O(n²) DP approach to LIS

sections.text: 1. Recap: Back to One Index, New Twist

House Robber's dp[i] only ever looked back at dp[i-1] and dp[i-2] — two fixed neighbors. LIS is 1D again (one index, one array), but dp[i] must consider EVERY earlier index j from 0 to i-1, not just the last one or two.
dp[i] represents the length of the longest increasing subsequence that ENDS exactly at index i (using arr[i] as its last element).

This 'must end here' framing is the key idea — it's different from House Robber's dp[i], which was the best answer using houses 0 through i in general, not one required to end at house i specifically.

sections.title: Tracing the dp array
points: dp = [1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1] to start — every element alone is length 1
dp[3] (value 5): only 2 (index 2) is smaller and earlier -> dp[3] = dp[2]+1 = 2
dp[5] (value 7): 2, 5, and 3 are smaller and earlier; best is dp[3]=2 or dp[4]=2, so dp[5] = 3
dp[6] (value 101): every earlier value is smaller; best earlier dp is dp[5]=3, so dp[6] = 4
The overall best across the whole dp array is 4 — e.g. the subsequence 2, 3, 7, 101 or 2, 5, 7, 101

sections.text: This is O(n²): for each index i, the inner loop checks every earlier index j. Unlike House Robber's fixed dp[i-1]/dp[i-2] lookback, LIS's best extension could come from ANY earlier index — there's no way to know which one without checking them all (a faster O(n log n) approach exists using binary search, but the O(n²) DP is the foundational version to master first).

sections.title: Finding the longest stretch of years with rising sales, skipping bad years
sections.text: Looking at a company's yearly sales figures and finding the longest run of years (not necessarily consecutive calendar years) where each chosen year's sales beat the previous chosen year's is exactly Longest Increasing Subsequence — at each year, the best streak ending there depends on the best streak ending at any earlier, lower-selling year.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to initialize every dp[i] to 1 — every single element is trivially an increasing subsequence of length 1 on its own.
Returning dp[n-1] instead of the maximum across the WHOLE dp array — unlike House Robber, the longest subsequence doesn't necessarily end at the last index.
Using <= instead of < when comparing arr[j] and arr[i] — LIS here is strictly increasing, so equal values don't extend the subsequence.
Confusing dp[i] = 'best subsequence using indices 0..i' with the correct meaning 'best subsequence ending exactly at index i' — mixing these up breaks the recurrence.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does dp[i] represent in the LIS problem?
questions.answer: The length of the longest increasing subsequence that ends exactly at index i, using arr[i] as its last element.

questions.question: What is the LIS recurrence?
questions.answer: dp[i] = max(dp[j] + 1) for every j < i where arr[j] < arr[i]; dp[i] = 1 if no such j exists.

questions.question: Why does the final answer require scanning the whole dp array instead of just returning dp[n-1]?
questions.answer: Because the longest increasing subsequence doesn't have to end at the last element — the maximum could occur at any index, so every dp[i] must be checked.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Longest Increasing Subsequence uses 1D DP with dp[i] defined as the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending exactly at index i. For each i, an inner loop checks every earlier index j: if arr[j] < arr[i], arr[i] could extend that subsequence, giving a candidate length of dp[j] + 1, and dp[i] takes the best such candidate (or 1, if no smaller earlier element exists). This double loop makes the approach O(n²). Unlike House Robber, the final answer isn't simply the last entry — since the longest subsequence can end anywhere, the answer is the maximum value found across the entire dp array.

sections.title: Trick to Remember LIS
sections.text: LIS: dp[i] = best subsequence ENDING at i. Check every earlier smaller element, extend the best one, +1. Answer = max of the whole dp array, not just the last cell.

sections.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.
hints: This is the lesson's own worked example — try implementing it yourself before checking back against the full trace above.
State: dp[i] = length of the LIS ending exactly at index i.
Recurrence: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) over every j < i where nums[j] < nums[i]; the final answer is the max across the whole dp array.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: The O(n^2) dp recurrence directly, this lesson's own worked example implemented from scratch.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Patience sorting with binary search — maintain the smallest possible tail for each subsequence length.
